strategy_candidates.title: Step-by-Step Writing Flow
strategy_candidates.short_description: A chronological breakdown of the user's journey through the DiaryMate interface.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The text explicitly mentions a 'procedure' and 'writing flow.' Breaking this down into a numbered list of actions helps the user visualize the system in use.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Using the 'procedure' mentioned in the text and the surrounding context, describe the user's interaction with DiaryMate as a sequence of 3-4 specific steps, starting from entering a title.

strategy_candidates.title: Guided vs. Unguided in Action
strategy_candidates.short_description: A side-by-side comparison of how a single diary entry would look in both modes.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The user's persistent confusion suggests that the conceptual definitions of 'guided' and 'unguided' are not sticking. A concrete scenario provides a mental anchor.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Create a hypothetical scenario where a user wants to write about 'a busy day at work.' Show exactly what the user would do and what the AI would provide in 'User-guided' mode versus 'Unguided' mode.

strategy_candidates.title: The Interaction Logic
strategy_candidates.short_description: A map of what the user provides and what the system generates.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: This strategy clarifies the 'design goals' by showing the technical relationship between user input (keywords, text) and AI output.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Identify the specific inputs the user provides (like keywords or titles) and explain how the system processes these to produce the 'next sentences' or 'sentence generation' mentioned in the text.

strategy_candidates.title: The 'GPS vs. Paper Map' Analogy
strategy_candidates.short_description: Reframing the authoring modes using a familiar navigation analogy.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: When technical explanations fail, a non-academic analogy can bypass the cognitive block by relating the concept to everyday experience.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Explain the difference between guided and unguided authoring using an analogy, such as the difference between following a GPS (guided) and using a paper map to find your own way (unguided).

strategy_candidates.title: The 'Big Picture' in Plain English
strategy_candidates.short_description: Strip away the technical terms to explain the core concept of the writing flow.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The user is frustrated and has asked for more explanation twice. Simplifying the language helps reset their mental model without the burden of academic jargon like 'instantiated' or 'authoring capabilities'.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Explain the selected passage using only everyday language. Avoid using the words 'instantiated', 'authoring', or 'capabilities'. Focus on the simple idea that the researchers built a tool that lets users choose between 'AI-led' and 'User-led' writing.
expected_answer_shape: A one-sentence summary of the goal.
A simple contrast between the two modes.
A brief mention of why this choice matters for the user.

strategy_candidates.title: The 'GPS vs. Paper Map' Analogy
strategy_candidates.short_description: Use a familiar real-world comparison to explain the difference between guided and unguided writing.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: Analogies are effective for breaking through frustration by anchoring new, complex information to something the user already understands perfectly.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Compare the 'writing flow' described in the text to using a GPS (guided) versus using a paper map (unguided). Explain how the researchers designed DiaryMate to allow for both, depending on how much help the 'driver' (the writer) wants at that moment.
expected_answer_shape: A mapping of 'guided' to GPS and 'unguided' to a map.
A concluding sentence on how DiaryMate combines both.

strategy_candidates.title: The Bottom Line
strategy_candidates.short_description: Start with the most important conclusion of this section before explaining the procedure.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: When frustration is high, users often lose track of 'why' they are reading a specific detail. Providing the 'so what' first can provide the necessary motivation to parse the procedure.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Start by explaining that this sentence is the 'instruction manual' for the study. Then, explain that the most important thing to know is that the researchers wanted to see if people prefer the AI to be a 'suggestive partner' or just a 'blank page' assistant.
expected_answer_shape: A brief explanation of how this takeaway relates to the 'writing flow' mentioned in the text.

strategy_candidates.title: Focus on the First Step
strategy_candidates.short_description: Ignore the complex setup and focus only on the very first action the user takes in the system.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The passage mentions a 'procedure' is about to follow. Reducing information density by focusing only on the immediate next step (entering a title/content) can make the system feel less complex.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Focus only on the 'First' step mentioned at the end of the passage. Explain that despite the complex 'guided/unguided' talk, the process starts exactly like a normal diary: the user just types. Explain why starting 'normally' is important for the researchers' goals.
expected_answer_shape: A description of the first step of the procedure.
An explanation of why this 'normal' start helps the user feel in control.

strategy_candidates.title: The 'Ignore or Request' Choice
strategy_candidates.short_description: Discuss the implications of the user's power to reject AI suggestions.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The caption highlights that users can 'ignore' or 'request a new' sentence. This is central to the paper's theme of human-AI collaboration and user agency.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Focus on the last sentence of the caption regarding the user's choice to incorporate, ignore, or request new text. Explain why this specific 'human-in-the-loop' design is critical for a personal task like journaling and how it relates to the paper's later findings on user over-reliance.

strategy_candidates.title: Journaling vs. General Writing Assistants
strategy_candidates.short_description: Compare this flow to standard AI writing tools like Gmail Smart Compose.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The paper argues that journaling requires a 'different kind of assistance.' Comparing this flow to more familiar tools helps contextualize the researchers' design choices.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Compare the interaction flow shown in Figure 2 with standard predictive text or 'Smart Compose' features found in email or document editors. Highlight what makes the DiaryMate flow specifically tailored for 'reflective and personal' contexts versus simple productivity.

strategy_candidates.title: Critique the 'Suggested Next Sentence'
strategy_candidates.short_description: Question whether AI-suggested sentences might interfere with the 'introspective' goal of the paper.
strategy_candidates.why_recommended: The paper profile mentions a risk of over-reliance. An engaged user is well-positioned to critique the assumption that AI suggestions actually aid reflection.
strategy_candidates.prompt_instruction: Critically examine the 'Suggested next sentences' feature shown in the diagram. Based on the paper's goal of 'introspective' writing, discuss the potential conflict between having an AI predict a user's next thought and the user's own process of self-discovery.
